title: Fabrication and transfer of ultra-thin YBa$2$Cu$3$O$_{7-x}$ film on SrTiO$_3$ nanomembrane
authors: J. S. Madhira, G. Potemkin, D. Grützmacher, T. Schäpers, M. Lyatti
abstract: The fabrication of free-standing ultra-thin films from high-temperature (high-Tc) superconductors is of great interest for the development of superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors with high operating temperatures. We successfully fabricate a millimeter-sized, high-quality, ultra-thin YBa$2$Cu$3$O$_{7-x}$ (YBCO) film on an SrTiO$_3$/Sr$_{1.5}$Ca$_{1.5}$Al$_2$O$_6$ (STO/SCAO) bilayer using high-pressure sputtering. The STO nanomembranes with the YBCO films are released by dissolving the water-soluble SCAO sacrificial layer and transferred onto the SiO2/Si substrate. X-ray diffraction confirms that STO and YBCO crystallinity is preserved following transfer onto SiO2/Si substrates. Microbridges patterned from the transferred YBCO films exhibit a critical temperature of 88.8 K and a critical current density of 6.8 MA/cm2 at 77 K, demonstrating robust superconducting transport after transfer. The thermal boundary conductance across the van der Waals interface between the STO nanomembrane with YBCO film and the SiO2/Si substrate, measured over 15-75 K, is significantly reduced compared to that of epitaxial YBCO film on bulk STO leading to modified energy relaxation and enhanced stability of transient resistive states. These results establish ultra-thin YBCO films on optically transparent STO nanomembranes as a platform for integrating high-Tc superconducting devices with SiO2/Si-based photonic structures.
summary: The researchers developed a way to transfer ultra-thin high-temperature superconducting YBCO films onto silicon substrates using a nanomembrane technique. This process preserves the material's high-quality superconductivity and creates a thermal barrier that could improve the performance of single-photon detectors.
main_problem: The need for a scalable fabrication method to integrate high-Tc superconductors like YBCO with standard silicon-based photonic architectures for applications like superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors.
main_result: Successful fabrication and transfer of ultra-thin YBCO films on STO nanomembranes to SiO2/Si substrates, maintaining high crystallinity and robust superconducting properties (Tc = 88.8 K) while significantly reducing thermal boundary conductance.
method: High-pressure sputtering of YBCO/Au on an STO/SCAO bilayer, followed by the dissolution of the water-soluble SCAO sacrificial layer and a controlled transfer process using a pre-conditioned DGL polymer film.
model_or_system: An ultra-thin (20 nm) YBa2Cu3O7-x (YBCO) film on a SrTiO3 (STO) nanomembrane, transferred onto a SiO2/Si substrate, forming a van der Waals interface.
key_observables: Critical temperature (Tc), critical current density (Jc), thermal boundary conductance (TBC), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) lattice constants.
important_parameters: YBCO thickness ~20 nm, Tc = 88.8 K, Jc = 6.8 MA/cm2 at 77 K, and TBC reduction of nearly two orders of magnitude.
main_assumptions: The reduction in thermal boundary conductance is assumed to be the primary driver for enhanced stability of transient resistive states in the nanowires.
figures_summary: XRD scans of STO and the full YBCO/STO stack, AFM images of the surface, and the experimental procedure for the DGL-mediated transfer process.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the motivation for high-Tc SNSPDs, describes the fabrication and transfer protocol, presents structural and electrical characterization of the transferred films, analyzes the thermal boundary conductance, and concludes with the implications for photonic integration.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. XRD 2Θ-Θ scans of (a) the STO/SCAO stack on the STO substrate and (b) the STO nanomembrane transferred onto the SiO2/Si substrate. Insert of panel (b) shows an optical image of the STO nanomembrane on the SiO2/Si substrate.

FIG. 2. XRD 2Θ-Θ scans of the Au/YBCO/STO/SCAO stack on the STO substrate (a) and the Au/YBCO/STO stack transferred onto the SiO2/Si substrate (b). Insert of panel (b) shows an optical image of the Au/YBCO/STO heterostructure transferred onto the SiO2/Si substrate.

FIG. 3. A false-color optical image of the Au/YBCO microbridges on the STO nanomembrane transferred onto the SiO2/Si substrate. The Au/YBCO film, the Au/YBCO microbridges, and the STO substrate are highlighted in green, blue, and yellow, respectively. Black dots are precipitates. The insert shows an optical image of a 10x10 mm SiO₂/Si chip with seven Au/YBCO microbridges on the STO nanomembrane, as well as gold current leads.

Figure 4b shows the typical IV curve of an 8.7-µm-wide Au/YBCO bridge on the SiO2/Si substrate with critical current Ic of 8.6 mA. The critical current is determined using the 10 µV criterion. The critical current density of the bridges with the thickness d is calculated as Jc = Ic/W∙d, where W and d are the width and the thickness of the microbridge, respectively. The average critical current density of the 20-nm-thick Au/YBCO microbridges at T = 77.5 K is 4.5±0.4 MA/cm2 for the YBCO film sputtered on the STO layer with a mixed SrO/TiO2 surface termination obtained after STO layer deposition (Sample 1).
